title: eslint-disable comment leaks into published dist output
description:
**File:** src/plugins/hello-world.ts → dist/plugins/hello-world.js (line 1)

The `// eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars` comment on line 8 of hello-world.ts is preserved in the compiled dist/plugins/hello-world.js output. This is a development-only annotation that has no meaning in the published package and adds noise for consumers reading the compiled output.

**Options to fix:**
1. Configure TypeScript to strip comments: add `"removeComments": true` to tsconfig.json compilerOptions
2. Restructure the code to avoid the eslint-disable (e.g., prefix unused param with underscore: `_config`)

Option 2 is preferred as it also addresses the root cause — the `config` parameter triggers the no-unused-vars rule because it's genuinely unused. Using `_config` is the TypeScript convention for intentionally unused parameters.
close_reason: Removed eslint-disable comments by using _config convention and eslint rule configuration

title: hello-world.ts: Two eslint-disable comments suggest rule configuration issue
description:
**File:** src/plugins/hello-world.ts, lines 3 and 8

Two eslint-disable comments are used:
- Line 3: `// eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-empty-object-type` for the empty `HelloWorldPluginConfig` interface
- Line 8: `// eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars` for the `config` parameter

Both are legitimate patterns in this codebase:
1. Empty interfaces reserved for future use are a valid design pattern
2. Optional parameters that exist for API compatibility are intentional

Rather than disabling rules inline, consider:
- For `no-empty-object-type`: Add a rule override in eslint.config.js to allow empty interfaces (or use `Record<string, never>` as the type)
- For `no-unused-vars`: Rename to `_config` which TypeScript/ESLint conventionally treats as intentionally unused

This would eliminate the inline suppressions and keep the source cleaner.
close_reason: Replaced inline eslint-disable with _config convention and allowInterfaces/argsIgnorePattern rule config

title: prepublishOnly uses bun instead of npm-compatible commands
description:
**File:** package.json, line 19

The `prepublishOnly` script uses `bun run clean && bun run build`:
```json
"prepublishOnly": "bun run clean && bun run build"
```

This script runs during `npm publish`, which means it will fail for anyone publishing with npm if they don't have bun installed. The `prepublishOnly` hook is triggered by npm's publish lifecycle, so it should use `npm run` (or just reference the commands directly) for maximum compatibility:

```json
"prepublishOnly": "npm run clean && npm run build"
```

Both `npm run` and `bun run` will work when bun is the runtime, but only `npm run` works when npm is the runtime. Since this is a published package, the publish lifecycle should be runtime-agnostic.

Alternatively, use the commands directly:
```json
"prepublishOnly": "rm -rf dist/ && tsc"
```
close_reason: Changed prepublishOnly to use npm run instead of bun run

title: package.json: Missing author, keywords, bugs, and homepage fields
description:
**File:** package.json

Several optional but recommended npm package metadata fields are missing:

- `author`: Should be "Brandon Dennis" (matches LICENSE copyright holder)
- `keywords`: Helps discoverability on npm (e.g., `["opencode", "plugin", "workflow", "automation"]`)
- `bugs`: Link to GitHub issues (`https://github.com/toady00/open-mardis-gras/issues`)
- `homepage`: Link to repo or docs

These aren't required for functionality but improve the npm listing and help users find/report issues.
close_reason: Added author, keywords, bugs, and homepage fields to package.json

title: hello-world.ts: No error handling for client.app.log failure
close_reason: Wrapped client.app.log in try/catch for graceful failure

title: Spec: Initial Scaffolding for OpenCode Plugin
description:
# Initial Scaffolding Specification

## Overview

Create the foundational project structure for `open-mardi-gras`, a TypeScript-based OpenCode plugin that provides workflow automation features (starting with command chaining/return functionality). This scaffolding establishes the npm package structure, build system, TypeScript configuration, linting, and open source files required for publishing to the npm registry.

**IMPORTANT**: The `.opencode/` directory contains OpenCode's configuration and helper plugins for development purposes ONLY. It is NOT part of this plugin package and should be completely ignored in the published npm package.

**Goal**: Produce a working npm package that exports OpenCode plugins and can be installed via `npm install open-mardi-gras` or referenced in `opencode.json`.

## Requirements

### 1. Package Configuration

**1.1 Root package.json**
- Package name: `open-mardi-gras`
- Initial version: `0.1.0`
- Description: "OpenCode plugin for workflow automation and command chaining"
- Type: `module` (ES modules)
- Main entry: `./dist/index.js`
- Types entry: `./dist/index.d.ts`
- Files to include: `["dist/", "LICENSE", "README.md", "CHANGELOG.md"]` (explicitly excludes `.opencode/`)
- Scripts needed:
  - `build`: Compile TypeScript to `dist/` (`tsc`)
  - `lint`: Run ESLint (`eslint src/`)
  - `lint:fix`: Fix ESLint issues (`eslint src/ --fix`)
  - `clean`: Remove `dist/` directory (`rm -rf dist/`)
  - `prepublishOnly`: Run `clean` and `build` (clean must run first to avoid stale files)
  - `dev` (optional): Watch mode (`tsc --watch`)
- Engines field:
  - `node`: ">=18.0.0" (supports current LTS)

**1.2 Dependencies**
- `@opencode-ai/plugin`: `^1.2.10` (devDependency only - OpenCode guarantees it's available at runtime)
- `typescript`: `^5.x` (devDependency)
- `@types/node`: `^20.x` (devDependency)
- `eslint`: `^9.x` (devDependency)
- `@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin`: `^8.x` (devDependency)
- `@typescript-eslint/parser`: `^8.x` (devDependency)

### 2. TypeScript Configuration

**2.1 tsconfig.json**
- Target: ES2022
- Module: NodeNext
- ModuleResolution: NodeNext
- Declaration: true (generate .d.ts files)
- DeclarationMap: true
- SourceMap: true
- Strict: true
- OutDir: `./dist`
- RootDir: `./src`
- SkipLibCheck: true
- EsModuleInterop: true
- Exclude: `[".opencode/"]` (don't compile dev helpers)

### 3. ESLint Configuration

**3.1 eslint.config.js**
- Use ESLint flat config format (eslint.config.js)
- Include `@typescript-eslint/recommended` rules
- Include `@typescript-eslint/recommended-type-checked` rules
- Target files: `src/**/*.ts`
- Ignore: `dist/`, `node_modules/`, `.opencode/`
- Include parserOptions with project reference to tsconfig.json for type-aware linting

### 4. Project Structure

```
open-mardi-gras/
├── package.json           # NPM package configuration
├── tsconfig.json          # TypeScript compiler configuration
├── eslint.config.js       # ESLint configuration
├── LICENSE                # MIT license file
├── CHANGELOG.md           # Changelog following common-changelog format
├── README.md              # Existing - enhance with development info
├── .gitignore             # Add dist/, node_modules/
├── src/
│   ├── index.ts           # Main entry point - exports HelloWorldPlugin
│   └── plugins/
│       └── hello-world.ts # Validation plugin (hello world)
└── dist/                  # Compiled output (gitignored)
```

**Note**: `.opencode/` directory exists at root but is NOT part of the plugin package.

### 5. Source Code Files

**5.1 src/plugins/hello-world.ts**
- Simple "Hello World" OpenCode plugin to validate wiring
- Implements the Plugin type from `@opencode-ai/plugin`
- Logs a message on initialization
- Accepts optional configuration object (even if unused) to support future configuration needs
- Example structure:
  ```typescript
  import type { Plugin } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"
  
  export interface HelloWorldPluginConfig {
    // Reserved for future configuration options
  }
  
  export const HelloWorldPlugin = (config?: HelloWorldPluginConfig): Plugin => {
    return async ({ client }) => {
      await client.app.log({
        body: {
          service: "open-mardi-gras",
          level: "info",
          message: "HelloWorldPlugin initialized",
        },
      })
      
      return {
        // Empty hooks object - this plugin just validates wiring
      }
    }
  }
  ```

**5.2 src/index.ts**
- Main entry point for the plugin package
- Export HelloWorldPlugin from `./plugins/hello-world.js`
- Export HelloWorldPluginConfig type for TypeScript users
- Re-export types from `@opencode-ai/plugin` if needed
- Structure:
  ```typescript
  export { HelloWorldPlugin } from './plugins/hello-world.js'
  export type { HelloWorldPluginConfig } from './plugins/hello-world.js'
  export type * from '@opencode-ai/plugin'
  ```

### 6. Open Source Files

**6.1 LICENSE**
- MIT License
- Include copyright notice: "Copyright (c) 2026 Brandon Dennis"
- Use standard MIT license text

**6.2 CHANGELOG.md**
- Follow [Common Changelog](https://common-changelog.org/) format
- Initial version: 0.1.0
- Sections: "0.1.0 - 2026-02-26" with "Initial release" description
- Include link to full git history for details

**6.3 README.md (Enhancements)**
Add sections:
- Installation (npm install)
- Development setup (bun install, build, lint)
- Plugin usage in opencode.json (both string reference and import with config)
  ```typescript
  // Option 1: String reference (simplest)
  plugins: ['open-mardi-gras']
  
  // Option 2: Import with configuration (for advanced use)
  import { HelloWorldPlugin } from 'open-mardi-gras'
  plugins: [HelloWorldPlugin({ /* options */ })]
  ```
- Contributing (brief)
- License reference
- Changelog reference

### 7. Build System

**7.1 Compilation**
- Use `tsc` for building (no bundler needed for now)
- Output to `dist/` directory
- Include .d.ts files for TypeScript consumers
- Include source maps for debugging
- Must NOT compile `.opencode/` directory

**7.2 Watch Mode**
- `dev` script using `tsc --watch`

### 8. Git Configuration

**8.1 .gitignore additions**
```
# Build output
dist/

# Dependencies
node_modules/

# OS
.DS_Store

# Temporary files
*.tmp
*.swp
```

**8.2 Do NOT add to .gitignore**
- `.opencode/` - this directory IS tracked in git (contains dev helpers)

**8.3 npm publish exclusions**
- Use `files` field in package.json: `["dist/", "LICENSE", "README.md", "CHANGELOG.md"]`
- This explicitly excludes `.opencode/` from npm package

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Running `bun install` installs all dependencies
- [ ] Running `bun run build` compiles TypeScript without errors
- [ ] Running `bun run lint` checks code style without critical errors
- [ ] Running `bun run lint:fix` fixes auto-fixable ESLint issues
- [ ] Running `bun run clean` removes the `dist/` directory
- [ ] Running `bun run dev` starts TypeScript watch mode
- [ ] `dist/` directory contains compiled `.js` and `.d.ts` files
- [ ] `dist/` contains `.js.map` source map files
- [ ] `dist/` contains `.d.ts.map` declaration map files
- [ ] `dist/index.js` is the main entry point
- [ ] `dist/index.d.ts` provides TypeScript definitions
- [ ] `tsconfig.json` is valid (running `tsc --noEmit` succeeds)
- [ ] `eslint.config.js` is valid (running `eslint --print-config src/index.ts` succeeds)
- [ ] LICENSE file exists with standard MIT text and copyright "Brandon Dennis"
- [ ] CHANGELOG.md exists following common-changelog format with date 2026-02-26
- [ ] README includes development instructions
- [ ] README documents both string and import plugin usage patterns
- [ ] Package can be linked locally with `bun link` for testing
- [ ] Running `npm pack` creates a valid tarball without errors
- [ ] Running `npm pack --dry-run` shows only expected files (dist/, LICENSE, README.md, CHANGELOG.md)
- [ ] Running `npm publish --dry-run` succeeds without errors
- [ ] `files` field in package.json correctly limits published files
- [ ] `.opencode/` directory is NOT included in the published npm package
- [ ] `.opencode/` directory IS tracked in git (not gitignored)
- [ ] `git check-ignore dist/` returns dist/ (is ignored)
- [ ] `git check-ignore node_modules/` returns node_modules/ (is ignored)
- [ ] @opencode-ai/plugin is NOT in dependencies or peerDependencies (devDependency only)
- [ ] HelloWorldPlugin can be imported and used in an OpenCode configuration
- [ ] Installing the package via `npm install` and importing it works without errors
- [ ] Plugin accepts optional configuration object (even if unused in v0.1.0)

## Edge Cases

### Bun vs npm Compatibility
- Use `bun` for development (as per OpenCode)
- Ensure package works with standard `npm`/`node` consumers
- Lock file: Use `bun.lock` (Bun's format)
- Windows compatibility: `clean` script uses `rm -rf` (Unix-only); Windows support deferred to future release

### ESM-Only Package
- Do not generate CommonJS output
- Set `"type": "module"` in package.json
- Use `.js` extensions in imports (TypeScript handles this)
- Ensure `"module": "NodeNext"` in tsconfig.json

### Dependency Handling
- `@opencode-ai/plugin` is a devDependency only
- OpenCode guarantees runtime availability
- No peerDependencies complexity needed
- Type checking works during development

### Build Artifacts
- `dist/` is gitignored but included in npm package via `files` field
- Source maps included for debugging
- Declaration maps for IDE go-to-definition

### .opencode/ Directory Handling
- **In Git**: `.opencode/` IS tracked (helpers shared across dev environments)
- **In Build**: TypeScript excludes `.opencode/` from compilation
- **In npm**: `.opencode/` is NOT published (via `files` field in package.json)
- **In ESLint**: Ignored from linting (development helpers, not package code)

### Build Failures
- If TypeScript compilation fails, output must show file/line/column of errors
- Build process should exit with non-zero status code on failure
- `clean` script must complete successfully before `build` runs (order matters in prepublishOnly)

### Stale Build Artifacts
- `clean` script must remove entire `dist/` directory before rebuilding
- Stale files in dist/ must not be included in published package

### npm Package Verification
- `npm pack --dry-run` must show only expected files
- Published package must not include .opencode/, src/, or config files
- Package must be installable via `npm install` without errors

### Runtime Errors
- Plugin must fail gracefully if OpenCode client is unavailable
- Missing @opencode-ai/plugin at runtime should produce clear error message

## Dependencies

This epic has no dependencies. It is the foundational work.

## Child Issues

After this scaffolding is complete, the following child issues will be created:

1. **"Implement Return Plugin"** (separate epic)
   - Depends on: This scaffolding epic
   - Implements the command chaining feature described in README
   - Adds return.ts to src/plugins/
   - Updates index.ts exports

## Next Steps After Scaffolding

1. Verify scaffolding acceptance criteria all pass
2. Close this epic
3. Create child epic: "Implement Return Plugin" (depends on this epic)
4. Test locally with `bun link`
5. Create npm account and publish v0.1.0
6. Update documentation with real npm install instructions

## References

- OpenCode Plugin Documentation: https://opencode.ai/docs/plugins/
- Bun Documentation: https://bun.sh/docs
- TypeScript ESM Guide: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/esm-node.html
- npm Package Publishing: https://docs.npmjs.com/packages-and-modules/contributing-packages-to-the-registry
- npm files field: https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v10/configuring-npm/package-json#files
- Common Changelog: https://common-changelog.org/
close_reason: All scaffolding complete. Package builds, lints, and packs cleanly. All 9 implementation beads and 7 review findings resolved. Ready for v0.1.0 publish.

title: Create ESLint configuration (eslint.config.js)
description:
## Task: Create ESLint configuration (eslint.config.js)

**Location:** `eslint.config.js`

**Requirements:**
- Use ESLint flat config format (eslint.config.js)
- Include `@typescript-eslint/recommended` rules
- Include `@typescript-eslint/recommended-type-checked` rules
- Target files: `src/**/*.ts`
- Ignore: `dist/`, `node_modules/`, `.opencode/`
- Include parserOptions with project reference to tsconfig.json for type-aware linting

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] eslint.config.js is valid JavaScript
- [ ] Running `eslint --print-config src/index.ts` succeeds
- [ ] Running `bun run lint` checks code style without critical errors
- [ ] Running `bun run lint:fix` fixes auto-fixable ESLint issues

**Edge Cases:**
- Type-aware linting requires parserOptions.project pointing to tsconfig.json
- Flat config format (not .eslintrc.json)

title: Create directory structure and source files
description:
## Task: Create directory structure and source files

**Locations:** 
- `src/index.ts`
- `src/plugins/hello-world.ts`

**Directory Structure to Create:**
```
src/
├── index.ts           # Main entry point - exports HelloWorldPlugin
└── plugins/
    └── hello-world.ts # Validation plugin
```

**src/plugins/hello-world.ts Requirements:**
- Simple "Hello World" OpenCode plugin to validate wiring
- Implements the Plugin type from `@opencode-ai/plugin`
- Logs a message on initialization using client.app.log()
- Accepts optional configuration object (HelloWorldPluginConfig interface)
- Returns empty hooks object

Code structure:
```typescript
import type { Plugin } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"

export interface HelloWorldPluginConfig {
  // Reserved for future configuration options
}

export const HelloWorldPlugin = (config?: HelloWorldPluginConfig): Plugin => {
  return async ({ client }) => {
    await client.app.log({
      body: {
        service: "open-mardi-gras",
        level: "info",
        message: "HelloWorldPlugin initialized",
      },
    })
    
    return {
      // Empty hooks object - this plugin just validates wiring
    }
  }
}
```

**src/index.ts Requirements:**
- Main entry point for the plugin package
- Export HelloWorldPlugin from `./plugins/hello-world.js`
- Export HelloWorldPluginConfig type for TypeScript users
- Re-export types from `@opencode-ai/plugin`
- Use `.js` extensions in imports (TypeScript handles this)

Code structure:
```typescript
export { HelloWorldPlugin } from './plugins/hello-world.js'
export type { HelloWorldPluginConfig } from './plugins/hello-world.js'
export type * from '@opencode-ai/plugin'
```

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Directory structure exists: `src/` and `src/plugins/`
- [ ] hello-world.ts implements Plugin type correctly
- [ ] index.ts exports HelloWorldPlugin and HelloWorldPluginConfig
- [ ] Code follows TypeScript strict mode
- [ ] Running `bun run build` compiles without errors
- [ ] `dist/` contains compiled `.js` and `.d.ts` files
- [ ] `dist/index.js` is the main entry point
- [ ] `dist/index.d.ts` provides TypeScript definitions

**Edge Cases:**
- ESM imports must use `.js` extensions (TypeScript rewrites these)
- Plugin accepts optional config object (even if unused in v0.1.0)
close_reason: Created src/index.ts and src/plugins/hello-world.ts. HelloWorldPlugin implements Plugin type correctly, logs on init, accepts optional config. index.ts re-exports all public API. bun run build compiles cleanly with .js, .d.ts, and .map files in dist/.

title: Create LICENSE file (MIT)
description:

**Requirements:**
- MIT License
- Copyright notice: "Copyright (c) 2026 Brandon Dennis"
- Standard MIT license text

MIT License template:
```
MIT License

Copyright (c) 2026 Brandon Dennis

Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy
of this software and associated documentation files (the "Software"), to deal
in the Software without restriction, including without limitation the rights
to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell
copies of the Software, and to permit persons to whom the Software is
furnished to do so, subject to the following conditions:

The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all
copies or substantial portions of the Software.

THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR
IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY,
FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE
AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER
LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM,
OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE
SOFTWARE.
```

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] LICENSE file exists at repository root
- [ ] Contains standard MIT license text
- [ ] Copyright year is 2026
- [ ] Copyright holder is "Brandon Dennis"
- [ ] Running `npm pack --dry-run` includes LICENSE file

**Edge Cases:**
- Ensure file is not gitignored
- File must be included in npm package via files field in package.json
close_reason: Created LICENSE file at repo root with standard MIT license text, copyright 2026 Brandon Dennis.

title: Create CHANGELOG.md following common-changelog format
description:
## Task: Create CHANGELOG.md following common-changelog format

**Location:** `CHANGELOG.md`

**Requirements:**
- Follow [Common Changelog](https://common-changelog.org/) format
- Initial version: 0.1.0
- Date: 2026-02-26
- Sections: "0.1.0 - 2026-02-26" with "Initial release" description
- Include link to full git history for details

Common Changelog Format Example:
```markdown
# Changelog

## 0.1.0 - 2026-02-26

Initial release of open-mardi-gras.

### Added
- Initial scaffolding with TypeScript build system
- HelloWorldPlugin for validation
- ESLint configuration with TypeScript support
- NPM package configuration for ESM-only distribution

See the full git history for detailed changes.
```

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] CHANGELOG.md exists at repository root
- [ ] Follows common-changelog format
- [ ] Version 0.1.0 with date 2026-02-26
- [ ] Running `npm pack --dry-run` includes CHANGELOG.md

**Edge Cases:**
- Ensure file is not gitignored
- File must be included in npm package via files field in package.json

description:
## Task: Update README.md with development documentation

**Location:** `README.md` (enhance existing file)

**Requirements:**
Add the following sections to the existing README:

1. **Installation**

2. **Development Setup**

title: Configure .gitignore for build artifacts
description:
## Task: Configure .gitignore for build artifacts

**Location:** `.gitignore` (update existing file)

**Requirements:**
Add the following entries to .gitignore:

```
# Build output
dist/

# Dependencies
node_modules/

# OS
.DS_Store

# Temporary files
*.tmp
*.swp
```

**Important:** Do NOT add `.opencode/` to .gitignore
- The `.opencode/` directory IS tracked in git (contains dev helpers)
- It is excluded from npm package via package.json files field
- It is excluded from TypeScript compilation via tsconfig.json exclude

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] .gitignore exists at repository root
- [ ] `dist/` is gitignored
- [ ] `node_modules/` is gitignored
- [ ] OS files (.DS_Store) are gitignored
- [ ] Temporary files (*.tmp, *.swp) are gitignored
- [ ] Running `git check-ignore dist/` returns dist/ (is ignored)
- [ ] Running `git check-ignore node_modules/` returns node_modules/ (is ignored)
- [ ] `.opencode/` is NOT gitignored (remains tracked)

**Edge Cases:**
- Ensure `.opencode/` stays tracked in git
- dist/ is gitignored but included in npm via files field
close_reason: Added dist/, node_modules/, .DS_Store, *.tmp, *.swp to .gitignore. Verified all patterns match and .opencode/ remains tracked.

title: Build and verify the package
description:
## Task: Build and verify the package

**Requirements:**
Run the full build and verification process:

1. **Build Process:**
   - Run `bun run clean` to remove stale dist/
   - Run `bun run build` to compile TypeScript
   - Verify dist/ contains:
     - Compiled `.js` files
     - `.d.ts` declaration files
     - `.js.map` source maps
     - `.d.ts.map` declaration maps

2. **Lint Verification:**
   - Run `bun run lint` - should pass without critical errors

3. **NPM Package Verification:**
   - Run `npm pack --dry-run` and verify only expected files shown:
     - dist/
     - LICENSE
     - README.md
     - CHANGELOG.md
   - Run `npm publish --dry-run` - should succeed
   - Verify `.opencode/` is NOT in the package

4. **Link Testing:**
   - Run `bun link` for local testing
   - Verify package can be imported

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] `bun run build` compiles TypeScript without errors
- [ ] `bun run lint` passes without critical errors
- [ ] `dist/` contains `.js`, `.d.ts`, `.js.map`, `.d.ts.map` files
- [ ] `dist/index.js` is the main entry point
- [ ] `dist/index.d.ts` provides TypeScript definitions
- [ ] `npm pack --dry-run` shows only expected files
- [ ] `npm publish --dry-run` succeeds without errors
- [ ] `.opencode/` directory is NOT included in npm package
- [ ] `bun link` works for local testing
- [ ] HelloWorldPlugin can be imported and used

**Edge Cases:**
- Clean must complete before build in prepublishOnly
- Verify no stale files in dist/
- Package must be installable via npm install

Lint passes clean. npm pack shows only dist/, LICENSE, README.md, CHANGELOG.md (no .opencode/). npm publish --dry-run succeeds. bun link registers package. HelloWorldPlugin imports and instantiates correctly.

title: Code review: Initial Scaffolding for OpenCode Plugin

## Task: Code review - Initial Scaffolding for OpenCode Plugin

**Description:**
Invoke the reviewer agent (@bd-reviewer) to perform a thorough code review of all changes in this epic. The reviewer will file beads for every finding using discovered-from links. Close this bead only when the review is complete.

**Scope:**
Review all files created in this epic:
- package.json
- tsconfig.json
- eslint.config.js
- src/index.ts
- src/plugins/hello-world.ts

- CHANGELOG.md

2. Address any findings filed as discovered-from beads
3. Verify all acceptance criteria pass
4. Close this bead when review is complete

title: Create package.json with NPM configuration
description:
## Task: Create package.json with NPM configuration

**Location:** `package.json`

**Requirements:**
- Package name: `open-mardi-gras`
- Initial version: `0.1.0`
- Description: "OpenCode plugin for workflow automation and command chaining"
- Type: `module` (ES modules)
- Main entry: `./dist/index.js`
- Types entry: `./dist/index.d.ts`
- Files to include: `["dist/", "LICENSE", "README.md", "CHANGELOG.md"]` (explicitly excludes `.opencode/`)
- Scripts needed:
  - `build`: Compile TypeScript to `dist/` (`tsc`)
  - `lint`: Run ESLint (`eslint src/`)
  - `lint:fix`: Fix ESLint issues (`eslint src/ --fix`)
  - `clean`: Remove `dist/` directory (`rm -rf dist/`)
  - `prepublishOnly`: Run `clean` and `build` (clean must run first to avoid stale files)
  - `dev` (optional): Watch mode (`tsc --watch`)
- Engines field:
  - `node`: ">=18.0.0"

**Dependencies (devDependencies only):**
- `@opencode-ai/plugin`: `^1.2.10`
- `typescript`: `^5.x`
- `@types/node`: `^20.x`
- `eslint`: `^9.x`
- `@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin`: `^8.x`
- `@typescript-eslint/parser`: `^8.x`

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] package.json is valid JSON
- [ ] All required fields present
- [ ] @opencode-ai/plugin is in devDependencies only (not dependencies or peerDependencies)
- [ ] files field limits published files correctly
- [ ] Running `bun install` installs all dependencies without errors

**Edge Cases:**
- Ensure `"type": "module"` for ESM-only package
- No CommonJS output
- Windows compatibility: clean script uses rm -rf (Unix-only); defer Windows support to future release

title: ESLint config uses legacy rules spread instead of flat config presets
description:
**File:** eslint.config.js, lines 20-21

The current config spreads rules from the legacy config objects:
```js
rules: {
  ...eslint.configs.recommended.rules,
  ...eslint.configs["recommended-type-checked"].rules,
}
```

While this works (lint passes clean), the canonical approach for ESLint flat config with typescript-eslint v8 is to use the flat config presets directly:
```js
import tseslint from 'typescript-eslint'

export default tseslint.config(
  ...tseslint.configs.recommendedTypeChecked,
  { languageOptions: { parserOptions: { project: './tsconfig.json' } } },
  { ignores: ['dist/', 'node_modules/', '.opencode/'] },
)
```

The `typescript-eslint` package (note: not `@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin`) provides a `config()` helper and flat config arrays that properly set up the parser, plugin, and rules together. The current approach works but may miss parser/plugin configuration that the flat presets include.

This is a nit — the current config functions correctly. But upgrading to the canonical pattern would be more maintainable and consistent with the typescript-eslint documentation.

**Note:** The `typescript-eslint` unified package is not currently in devDependencies. Adding it would replace both `@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin` and `@typescript-eslint/parser`.
close_reason: Acknowledged as nit. Current config works correctly. Deferring migration to typescript-eslint unified package to a future iteration.

title: Then Chaining
description:
# Then Chaining

Deterministic follow-up execution after OpenCode commands complete.

## Problem

OpenCode commands are one-shot: you run `/do-something`, the model responds, and the conversation continues with whatever the user or model decides to do next. There's no way to declaratively say "after this command finishes, always do X." This makes it impossible to build reliable multi-step workflows from composable command files.

## Solution

Introduce a `then` key in command frontmatter. When a command with a `then` value completes, the plugin automatically submits the `then` value as the next message in the conversation — before the user or model can interject. This creates deterministic, declarative command chaining.

## Frontmatter Syntax

### Single prompt

```yaml
---
description: Review the current PR
then: "Summarize your findings in 3 bullet points"
---
Review the open PR for correctness, style, and test coverage.
```

After the model finishes the review, the plugin injects `"Summarize your findings in 3 bullet points"` as the next user message. The model then responds to that prompt in the same session.

### Single command

```yaml
---
description: Analyze the codebase
then: "/generate-report"
---
Analyze the project structure and identify areas for improvement.
```

After analysis completes, the plugin fires `/generate-report` as if the user had typed it.

### Ordered sequence

```yaml
---
description: Full release workflow
then:
  - "Check for any uncommitted changes and report them"
  - "/run-tests"
  - "/bump-version"
  - "Summarize everything that happened above"
---
Prepare the project for a new release.
```

Each entry executes in order. The next entry fires only after the previous one has fully completed (the model has finished responding or the chained command has finished its own execution, including any nested `then` chains).

## Behavior

### Prompt entries (plain text)

Any `then` entry that does **not** start with `/` is treated as a prompt. It is submitted to the conversation as a user message. The model sees it and responds to it normally.

### Command entries (slash-prefixed)

Any `then` entry that starts with `/` is treated as a command invocation. It is executed as if the user typed that command. The command may itself have a `then` chain, which will execute before the parent chain continues to its next entry.

### Execution model

1. User invokes a command (e.g., `/release-workflow`).
2. The model processes the command's prompt and produces a response.
3. When the response is complete, the plugin checks whether the originating command had a `then` value.
4. If yes, the plugin processes the first entry:
   - **Prompt**: inject as a user message; wait for model to finish responding.
   - **Command**: execute the command; wait for full completion (including any nested `then` chains).
5. Move to the next entry. Repeat until the chain is exhausted.
6. Control returns to the normal conversation flow.

### Nested chains

Commands invoked via `then` may themselves define `then` chains. These nested chains execute depth-first: the inner chain completes fully before the outer chain advances to its next entry. This is a stack-based execution model.

**Example:**

`/step-one` has `then: /step-two`.
`/step-two` has `then: "validate the output"`.

Execution order:
1. `/step-one` runs
2. `/step-two` runs (from step-one's `then`)
3. `"validate the output"` runs (from step-two's `then`)
4. step-one's chain is now exhausted; done.

### Recursion guard

To prevent infinite loops (e.g., `/a` chains to `/b` which chains back to `/a`), the plugin enforces a maximum chain depth. When the limit is reached, the chain halts and the plugin logs a warning. The default depth limit is **10**. This is configurable via plugin options.

## Implementation Strategy

### Reading frontmatter

The plugin needs to read command markdown files to extract `then` values from YAML frontmatter. Commands live in `.opencode/commands/` as markdown files. The plugin reads these files at initialization and whenever commands are invoked, parsing the YAML frontmatter to extract the `then` key.

### Detecting command completion

The plugin uses the `command.execute.before` hook to know when a command is being invoked and to associate the command's `then` chain with the current session/message context. It then uses `chat.message` or the experimental message transform hooks to detect when the model has finished responding to that command, which is the signal to inject the next `then` entry.

### Injecting follow-up messages

When a `then` entry needs to fire, the plugin uses the client SDK to submit a new message to the active session. For prompts, this is a user message. For commands, the plugin triggers the command through the appropriate client API method.

### State tracking

The plugin maintains an in-memory map of active chains keyed by session ID. Each entry tracks:

- The full `then` sequence for the originating command
- The current index into that sequence
- The current chain depth (for recursion guarding)

When a chain completes or is interrupted, the entry is cleaned up.

### Synthetic message handling

When OpenCode completes a subtask-style command, it may inject its own synthetic follow-up messages (e.g., asking the model to summarize). The plugin should intercept and remove these synthetic messages when a `then` chain is active, so that the `then` entry takes precedence rather than competing with OpenCode's default behavior. The `experimental.chat.messages.transform` hook is the mechanism for this.

## Configuration

The plugin accepts the following options relevant to `then` chaining:

```typescript
interface ThenChainingConfig {
  /** Maximum depth for nested then chains. Default: 10 */
  maxDepth?: number

  /**
   * What to do when a command has no `then` key and OpenCode
   * injects a synthetic follow-up message.
   *
   * - "keep": leave OpenCode's default behavior alone
   * - "remove": strip the synthetic message silently
   * - "replace": replace with a custom prompt (see `defaultFollowUp`)
   *
   * Default: "keep"
   */
  syntheticMessageBehavior?: "keep" | "remove" | "replace"

  /**
   * Custom prompt to use when syntheticMessageBehavior is "replace".
   * Only meaningful when syntheticMessageBehavior is "replace".
   */
  defaultFollowUp?: string
}
```

## Edge Cases

### Empty `then` value

A `then` key with an empty string or empty array is treated as no-op. No chaining occurs.

### Invalid command references

If a `then` entry references a command that doesn't exist (e.g., `/nonexistent`), the plugin logs a warning and skips that entry, advancing to the next one in the sequence. The chain does not abort entirely.

### Session ends mid-chain

If the session is terminated while a chain is in progress, the chain is abandoned. No cleanup is required beyond dropping the in-memory state.

### Concurrent commands in the same session

If a user manually invokes a new command while a `then` chain is still executing, the chain is interrupted. The user's explicit action takes priority. The interrupted chain is discarded.

### Argument forwarding

`then` command entries can include arguments, e.g., `then: "/deploy staging"`. The arguments are passed through as if the user had typed them. The `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in the target command's template will receive `"staging"`.

## Non-Goals (for now)

- **Conditional chaining**: branching based on command output (e.g., "if tests pass, deploy; otherwise, fix"). This is a future consideration.
- **Parallel execution**: running multiple `then` entries concurrently. All entries execute sequentially.
- **Result capture / interpolation**: referencing the output of a previous chain step in a later step (e.g., `$RESULT[step-one]`). This is a future consideration.
- **Dynamic `then` values**: computing the `then` value at runtime based on command output. The `then` value is always read statically from frontmatter.

title: Parse then key from command frontmatter
description:
## Summary

Parse the `then` key from YAML frontmatter in OpenCode command markdown files. This is the foundational data layer that all other then-chaining tasks depend on.

## Location

Create `src/then-chaining/frontmatter.ts`.

## What to implement

A function that reads a command markdown file path and extracts the `then` value from its YAML frontmatter. The function must handle all three syntactic forms defined in the spec:

1. **Single string** — `then: "some prompt"` → returns `["some prompt"]`
2. **Single command** — `then: "/some-command"` → returns `["/some-command"]`
3. **Ordered array** — `then: ["a", "/b", "c"]` → returns `["a", "/b", "c"]`

### Function signature

```typescript
export interface ThenEntry {
  /** The raw string value from frontmatter */
  value: string
  /** Whether this entry is a command (starts with /) or a prompt */
  type: "command" | "prompt"
}

/**
 * Parse the `then` key from a command markdown file's YAML frontmatter.
 * Returns an empty array if no `then` key is present or the value is empty.
 */
export function parseThenChain(markdownContent: string): ThenEntry[]
```

### Parsing rules

- Use a lightweight YAML frontmatter parser. The frontmatter is delimited by `---` on its own line at the start of the file. Do NOT add a heavy dependency — use a simple regex-based extractor for the `---` delimiters, then parse the YAML block. Consider using the `js-yaml` package (already available as a transitive dep of eslint, but should be added as a direct dependency) or a minimal hand-rolled parser.
- Normalize single string values to a one-element array for uniform downstream handling.
- Classify each entry: if it starts with `/`, it is `type: "command"`; otherwise `type: "prompt"`.
- An empty string, `null`, `undefined`, or empty array `[]` for the `then` key should all return `[]` (no-op).
- Arguments in command entries (e.g., `"/deploy staging"`) are preserved as-is in the `value` field. Argument splitting is the responsibility of the executor, not the parser.

### Edge cases

- File has no frontmatter at all → return `[]`
- Frontmatter exists but has no `then` key → return `[]`
- `then` key is present but value is empty string or empty array → return `[]`
- `then` key has a single string value (not an array) → normalize to `[{ value, type }]`

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `parseThenChain` correctly handles all three syntactic forms
- [ ] Empty/missing `then` values return `[]`
- [ ] Each entry is classified as `"command"` or `"prompt"` based on `/` prefix
- [ ] No new heavy dependencies — use `js-yaml` (add as direct dep) or hand-roll
- [ ] Exported from `src/then-chaining/frontmatter.ts`
- [ ] TypeScript strict mode passes
- [ ] `bun run build` succeeds

title: Chain state manager
description:
## Summary

Implement the in-memory state tracker that manages active then-chains per session. This is the data structure that the executor reads from and writes to.

## Location

Create `src/then-chaining/state.ts`.

## What to implement

A `ChainStateManager` class that tracks active then-chains keyed by session ID. Each chain entry stores the full sequence, current position, and recursion depth.

### Data structures

```typescript
import type { ThenEntry } from "./frontmatter.js"

export interface ChainFrame {
  /** The full then sequence for this command invocation */
  entries: ThenEntry[]
  /** Current index into the entries array (0-based) */
  currentIndex: number
  /** The command name that originated this chain (for logging) */
  originCommand: string
}

export interface ChainState {
  /** Stack of chain frames — innermost (deepest nested) chain is last */
  stack: ChainFrame[]
}
```

### Class API

```typescript
export class ChainStateManager {
  private chains: Map<string, ChainState>
  private maxDepth: number

  constructor(maxDepth?: number)  // default 10

  /**
   * Push a new chain frame onto the stack for a session.
   * Returns false if max depth would be exceeded (recursion guard).
   */
  pushChain(sessionID: string, entries: ThenEntry[], originCommand: string): boolean

  /**
   * Get the next entry to execute for a session.
   * Returns undefined if no active chain or chain is exhausted.
   * Advances the current index.
   */
  advance(sessionID: string): ThenEntry | undefined

  /**
   * Pop the current (innermost) chain frame.
   * Called when a nested chain completes.
   * If the stack is empty after popping, cleans up the session entry.
   */
  popChain(sessionID: string): void

  /**
   * Check if a session has an active chain.
   */
  hasActiveChain(sessionID: string): boolean

  /**
   * Get the current depth (stack size) for a session.
   */
  currentDepth(sessionID: string): number

  /**
   * Interrupt and discard all chains for a session.
   * Called when user manually invokes a command mid-chain,
   * or when the session ends.
   */
  interrupt(sessionID: string): void
}
```

### Behavior details

- **Stack-based execution**: When a command in a then-chain itself has a `then` value, a new frame is pushed onto the stack. The executor processes the innermost frame first (depth-first). When a frame is exhausted, it is popped and the outer frame continues.
- **Recursion guard**: `pushChain` checks `stack.length < maxDepth` before pushing. If the limit would be exceeded, it returns `false` and the caller should log a warning and halt the chain.
- **Interruption**: `interrupt()` clears all state for a session. This is called when the user manually invokes a command while a chain is active, or when the session ends.
- **Cleanup**: When `popChain` is called and the stack becomes empty, the session entry is removed from the map entirely (no memory leak).

## Dependencies

- Depends on `ThenEntry` type from `src/then-chaining/frontmatter.ts` (omg-thw.1)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `ChainStateManager` correctly manages a stack of chain frames per session
- [ ] `pushChain` enforces max depth and returns false when exceeded
- [ ] `advance` returns entries in order and returns undefined when exhausted
- [ ] `popChain` removes exhausted frames and cleans up empty sessions
- [ ] `interrupt` clears all state for a session
- [ ] `hasActiveChain` and `currentDepth` return correct values
- [ ] No external dependencies — pure TypeScript data structure
- [ ] Exported from `src/then-chaining/state.ts`
- [ ] TypeScript strict mode passes
- [ ] `bun run build` succeeds

title: Chain executor
description:
## Summary

Implement the chain executor that processes then-chain entries sequentially, handling both prompt injection and command invocation. This is the core orchestration logic.

## Location

Create `src/then-chaining/executor.ts`.

## What to implement

A `ChainExecutor` class that takes the next entry from the state manager and dispatches it — either as a user message (prompt) or as a command invocation — using the OpenCode client SDK.

### Class API

```typescript
import type { PluginInput } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"
import type { ChainStateManager } from "./state.js"
import type { ThenEntry } from "./frontmatter.js"

export class ChainExecutor {
  /**
   * Set of session IDs where the executor is currently dispatching
   * a command. The command.execute.before hook checks this to
   * distinguish chain-invoked commands from user-invoked commands.
   *
   * - If sessionID is in this set when command.execute.before fires,
   *   the command was dispatched by the chain → push nested frame.
   * - If sessionID is NOT in this set, the command was user-initiated
   *   → interrupt the active chain first.
   *
   * The executor adds the sessionID before calling client.session.command()
   * and removes it in a finally block after the call completes.
   */
  readonly pendingDispatches: Set<string>

  constructor(
    private client: PluginInput["client"],
    private stateManager: ChainStateManager,
    private logger: (level: "info" | "warn" | "error", message: string) => Promise<void>
  )

  /**
   * Process the next entry in the active chain for a session.
   * Called when a command or prompt response completes.
   *
   * - If the next entry is a prompt: sends it as a user message via client.session.prompt()
   * - If the next entry is a command: executes it via client.session.command()
   *   (the command.execute.before hook will handle pushing any nested then-chain)
   * - If the chain is exhausted at the current depth: pops the frame and
   *   tries the next entry in the parent chain (recursive unwinding)
   * - If no more entries at any depth: chain is complete, state is cleaned up
   *
   * Returns true if an entry was dispatched, false if the chain is complete.
   */
  async processNext(sessionID: string): Promise<boolean>
}
```

### Execution logic

1. Call `stateManager.advance(sessionID)` to get the next entry.
2. If `undefined`:
   - Call `stateManager.popChain(sessionID)` to remove the exhausted frame.
   - If `stateManager.hasActiveChain(sessionID)` is still true, recurse (try the parent frame).
   - Otherwise, chain is fully complete. Return `false`.
3. If the entry is a **prompt** (`type === "prompt"`):
   - Call `client.session.prompt({ body: { sessionID, content: { type: "text", value: entry.value } } })`.
   - Log: `"Then chain: injecting prompt: \"${entry.value.substring(0, 50)}...\""`.
   - Return `true`.
4. If the entry is a **command** (`type === "command"`):
   - Split `entry.value` into command name and arguments. The command name is the first space-delimited token (including the leading `/`). Everything after the first space is the arguments string. Example: `"/deploy staging"` → command=`"/deploy"`, args=`"staging"`.
   - Add `sessionID` to `this.pendingDispatches`.
   - In a try/finally block:
     - Call `client.session.command({ body: { sessionID, command: commandName, arguments: args } })`.
   - In the finally block: remove `sessionID` from `this.pendingDispatches`.
   - Log: `"Then chain: executing command: ${entry.value}"`.
   - Return `true`.
   - Note: The `command.execute.before` hook (wired in the plugin task) will handle reading the invoked command's own `then` value and pushing it onto the state stack. The executor does not need to handle nested chain setup — that's the hook's job.

### Error handling

- If `client.session.prompt()` or `client.session.command()` throws, log the error and advance to the next entry (skip the failed one, matching the spec's behavior for invalid command references).
- If the command name doesn't exist, OpenCode will handle the error. The executor just logs and moves on.

## Dependencies

- Depends on `ChainStateManager` from `src/then-chaining/state.ts` (omg-thw.2)
- Depends on `ThenEntry` type from `src/then-chaining/frontmatter.ts` (omg-thw.1)
- Uses `@opencode-ai/sdk` client (available via `PluginInput`)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `processNext` dispatches prompt entries via `client.session.prompt()`
- [ ] `processNext` dispatches command entries via `client.session.command()` with correct argument splitting
- [ ] `pendingDispatches` Set is populated before command dispatch and cleared in finally block
- [ ] Exhausted frames are popped and parent frames are tried (recursive unwinding)
- [ ] Errors during dispatch are logged and the entry is skipped
- [ ] Returns `true` when an entry was dispatched, `false` when chain is complete
- [ ] Logging provides clear trace of chain execution
- [ ] Exported from `src/then-chaining/executor.ts`
- [ ] TypeScript strict mode passes
- [ ] `bun run build` succeeds

title: Synthetic message filter
description:
## Summary

Implement the `experimental.chat.messages.transform` hook handler that intercepts and removes OpenCode's synthetic follow-up messages when a then-chain is active. This prevents OpenCode's default behavior from competing with the then-chain's next entry.

## Location

Create `src/then-chaining/synthetic-filter.ts`.

## What to implement

A function that implements the `experimental.chat.messages.transform` hook. When a then-chain is active for the current session, it strips synthetic messages that OpenCode injects after subtask-style commands complete. When no chain is active, it respects the `syntheticMessageBehavior` config option.

### Function signature

```typescript
import type { Hooks } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"
import type { ChainStateManager } from "./state.js"

export type SyntheticMessageBehavior = "keep" | "remove" | "replace"

export interface SyntheticFilterConfig {
  behavior: SyntheticMessageBehavior
  defaultFollowUp?: string
}

/**
 * Create the experimental.chat.messages.transform hook handler.
 *
 * When a then-chain is active: always remove synthetic messages
 * (the then-chain entry takes precedence).
 *
 * When no then-chain is active: apply the configured behavior
 * ("keep", "remove", or "replace").
 */
export function createSyntheticFilter(
  stateManager: ChainStateManager,
  config: SyntheticFilterConfig,
  logger: (level: "info" | "warn" | "error", message: string) => Promise<void>
): NonNullable<Hooks["experimental.chat.messages.transform"]>
```

### Identifying synthetic messages

OpenCode injects synthetic user messages after subtask-style commands complete. These are identifiable by examining the message list — they appear as user messages that were not explicitly sent by the user. The exact heuristic may need refinement during implementation, but the approach is:

1. The hook receives the full message list in `output.messages`.
2. Look at the last message in the list. If it is a user message that appears immediately after an assistant response to a command, it is likely synthetic.
3. When a then-chain is active, remove this synthetic message from `output.messages` so the chain executor's injected message takes its place.
4. When no chain is active, apply the configured behavior:
   - `"keep"`: do nothing (leave the message)
   - `"remove"`: remove the synthetic message
   - `"replace"`: replace the synthetic message content with `config.defaultFollowUp`

### Important notes

- This hook does NOT know the session ID from its input (the `input` object is `{}`). The state manager needs to be checked for ANY active session. In practice, only one session is active at a time in OpenCode, so checking `stateManager` for any active chain is sufficient.
- The hook mutates `output.messages` in place (this is the plugin SDK's convention for transform hooks).
- Be conservative: if unsure whether a message is synthetic, leave it alone. False positives (removing real user messages) are worse than false negatives.

## Dependencies

- Depends on `ChainStateManager` from `src/then-chaining/state.ts` (omg-thw.2)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Synthetic messages are removed when a then-chain is active
- [ ] When no chain is active, `syntheticMessageBehavior` config is respected
- [ ] `"keep"` leaves messages untouched
- [ ] `"remove"` strips synthetic messages
- [ ] `"replace"` substitutes with `defaultFollowUp` content
- [ ] Does not remove non-synthetic user messages
- [ ] Exported from `src/then-chaining/synthetic-filter.ts`
- [ ] TypeScript strict mode passes
- [ ] `bun run build` succeeds

title: Plugin wiring and config
Module barrel export
description:
## Summary

Create the barrel export file for the `then-chaining` module directory and ensure all public types and functions are properly exported from the package.

## Location

- Create `src/then-chaining/index.ts`
- Update `src/index.ts`

## What to implement

### `src/then-chaining/index.ts`

Re-export all public types and functions from the module's files:

```typescript
export { parseThenChain } from "./frontmatter.js"
export type { ThenEntry } from "./frontmatter.js"

export { ChainStateManager } from "./state.js"
export type { ChainFrame, ChainState } from "./state.js"

export { ChainExecutor } from "./executor.js"

export { createSyntheticFilter } from "./synthetic-filter.js"
export type { SyntheticMessageBehavior, SyntheticFilterConfig } from "./synthetic-filter.js"
```

### `src/index.ts`

The final `src/index.ts` should export:

```typescript
// Plugin factories
export { HelloWorldPlugin } from "./plugins/hello-world.js"
export type { HelloWorldPluginConfig } from "./plugins/hello-world.js"
export { ThenChainingPlugin } from "./plugins/then-chaining.js"
export type { ThenChainingConfig } from "./plugins/then-chaining.js"

// Re-export SDK types for convenience
export type * from "@opencode-ai/plugin"
```

The internal `then-chaining` module types do NOT need to be re-exported from the package root — they are implementation details. Only the plugin factory and its config are public API.

## Dependencies

- Depends on all four implementation tasks being complete:
  - omg-thw.1 (frontmatter parser)
  - omg-thw.2 (state manager)
  - omg-thw.3 (executor)
  - omg-thw.4 (synthetic filter)
- Depends on omg-thw.5 (plugin wiring) since it updates `src/index.ts`

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `src/then-chaining/index.ts` re-exports all public types and functions
- [ ] `src/index.ts` exports both `HelloWorldPlugin` and `ThenChainingPlugin`
- [ ] TypeScript strict mode passes
- [ ] `bun run build` succeeds
- [ ] `bun run lint` passes

title: Code review: Then Chaining
description:
Invoke the reviewer agent (@bd-reviewer) to perform a thorough code review of all changes in this epic. The reviewer will file beads for every finding using discovered-from links. Close this bead only when the review is complete.

## Review scope

All files created or modified under this epic:

- `src/then-chaining/frontmatter.ts` — frontmatter parser
- `src/then-chaining/state.ts` — chain state manager
- `src/then-chaining/executor.ts` — chain executor
- `src/then-chaining/synthetic-filter.ts` — synthetic message filter
- `src/then-chaining/index.ts` — barrel exports
- `src/plugins/then-chaining.ts` — plugin factory and hook wiring
- `src/index.ts` — updated package exports

## Review checklist

- [ ] All spec requirements are implemented (compare against epic body)
- [ ] Edge cases from the spec are handled (empty then, invalid commands, session end, concurrent commands, argument forwarding)
- [ ] TypeScript strict mode compliance
- [ ] No unnecessary dependencies added
- [ ] Error handling is robust (no unhandled promise rejections)
- [ ] Logging is consistent and useful for debugging
- [ ] Config defaults match the spec
- [ ] Recursion guard works correctly
- [ ] State cleanup prevents memory leaks
- [ ] Code is well-structured and follows existing patterns
